Predicting pedestrian violating crossing.claim_text: Instrumental attitude is a significant predictor of pedestrians' violating crossing behavior intention across all models tested.
Descriptive norm (perceived actual behavior of family and friends) is a stronger predictor than subjective norm (perceived approval) and remains significant when added to the basic TPB model.

Conformity tendency (the tendency to modify behavior when around others) is a strong predictor of violating crossing intention, indicating that the presence of other pedestrians influences behavioral intention.

Subjective norm is significant in the basic TPB model but becomes non-significant when descriptive norm is added, suggesting that observation of others' behavior has greater influence than perceived approval from important referents.
Perceived behavioral control is not a significant predictor of violating crossing behavior, likely because the behavior is not challenging enough for the study population.
